techniques.evidence_quote: They were mostly aimed at individuals to phish for cryptocurrency exchange credentials and general spam.
A CreateUser call was made for a new account DangerDev@protonmail.me... The TA manually created a user account called ses.
Additionally the TA created access keys for existing accounts... CreateKeyPair

The TA used an interesting technique to achieve persistent access into the AWS account... the creation of a role that allows identities from an external AWS account to assume a privileged role in the victim tenant.
After this action the TA performed a CreateLoginProfile call which is used to give a user the ability to login through the AWS management console.
The AdministratorAccess policy was attached to the newly created account with AttachUserPolicy.
Using AttachRolePolicy the TA added the AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy to the AssumeRole that allows for external access
After the creation of both roles, an AssumeRole event was observed... The TA assumed the role from their own account (671050157472).

techniques.technique_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
techniques.evidence_quote: The username ses is interesting, because it mimics accounts automatically created when using SES... the roleName is AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles which was very similar to an existing role name... AWSeservedSSO_* vs. AWSReservedSSO_*

Removing IAM users with DeleteUser, Cleaning up policies with DetachUserPolicy and DeleteUserPolicy, Deactivating long term access keys with UpdateAccessKey, Cleaning up long term access keys with DeleteAccessKey
Creating a LightSail instance upon discovery with CreateInstances
UpdateLoginProfile where the TA uses the initially compromised account to update the console password of another account.
Access to the environment was achieved through an accidentally exposed long term access key belonging to an IAM user. The access key belonged to a user with Administrator Access.

Repeated calls were made towards SES with GetSendQuota and ListIdentities. These calls are used to get an idea on how much emails can be sent at once and which emails and domains are registered to send emails.
After approximately two weeks the TA came back and ran ListUsers to list the IAM users in the AWS account.
The majority of this activity relates to EC2 instances... DescribeSecurityGroups event which lists all security groups in the account
ListGroupsForUser, ListInstanceProfiles, ListSSHPublicKeys
SimulatePrincipalPolicy... the TA used this service to test certain actions... they were interested in actions related to the SSM service and AWS Secrets Manager.
ListBuckets
The TA launched three instances with instance type p3.16xlarge... This instance type is much better for crypto mining as it has a GPU with 128GB and 64 vCPUs.
Four domains were created with RegisterDomain... login.3dlntl-paypal.com, paypal-lntl.com, 3dlntl-paypal.com...
